elements.sent-2411-001: Large language models demonstrate exceptional performance in simple code generation tasks but still face challenges in tackling complex problems.
elements.sent-2411-002: These challenges may stem from insufficient reasoning and problem decomposition capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this issue, we propose a reasoning-augmented data generation process, SRA-MCTS, which guides the model to autonomously generate high-quality intermediate reasoning paths.
elements.sent-2411-004: This creates a positive feedback loop, enabling continuous improvement.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our method operates entirely through the model itself without requiring additional supervision.
elements.sent-2411-006: By synthesizing natural language reasoning paths and translating them into executable code, the approach ensures analytical accuracy and enhances the success rate in solving complex tasks.
elements.sent-2411-007: Experimental results show that, even without additional supervisory signals, our method achieves performance improvements across different model scales, demonstrating the significant potential of self-improvement in small models.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, the method remains robust when traditional Chain-of-Thought (CoT) approaches exhibit performance degradation, with notable improvements observed in diversity metrics such as pass@10.
elements.sent-2411-009: We encourage further exploration of reasoning processes within training data to enhance the ability of language models to address complex problems.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{intro.png} \caption{The figure compares the impact of different reasoning augmentation methods on code benchmarks. The "Instruct" represents the officially released model, "CoT" refers to fine-tuned model using CoT data, and "SRA-MCTS" is our fine-tuned model using SRA-MCTS generated data.} \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-010: Large language models(LLMs) perform well on simple and medium-level problems in the field of code generation, but the performance on more complex problems is not satisfactory~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/LuoX0SGHT0LJ24,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2305-10679 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Some approaches enhance the question part of datasets by using GPT3.5~\cite{DBLP:conf/nips/Ouyang0JAWMZASR22 to refine open-source datasets or summarize open-source code, thereby increasing the depth and breadth of the questions~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/LuoX0SGHT0LJ24,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2312-02120 .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, merely improving the depth and breadth of the questions in the data may lead to high-quality questions, but without effectively enhancing the answers, it cannot ensure the correctness of the responses.
elements.sent-2411-013: Some work enhances the answers in the data by incorporating a natural language intermediary process as part of the response, guiding the model to generate results in the correct direction.
elements.sent-2411-014: Some use GPT3.5 to generate natural language solutions in a step-by-step Chain-of-Thought (CoT) format~\cite{DBLP:conf/acl/Wang0DWZDXWZC24 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Some simply introduce guiding ideas to steer the process~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2305-10679 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The previous work aims to guide the large model’s reasoning process toward generating correct results through natural language as an intermediary.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, the inherent uncertainty in the model’s generation process means that errors may still arise during the formulation of these natural language ideas.
elements.sent-2411-018: The experiments conducted by ScaleAI provide concrete experimental validation for previous work on answers that providing LLMs with correct solutions in natural language as a part of the answer, even if incomplete (just 10-20 tokens), can substantially boost the performance on benchmarks~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2409-03733 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In contrast, incorrect solutions result in a decline in solve rate.
elements.sent-2411-020: This demonstrates that providing solutions to large models can guide and inspire their reasoning process, and the correctness of the solution directly impacts the accuracy of the final result.
elements.sent-2411-021: We enhance the natural language in the answer section of the data and reduce the likelihood of errors in the intermediate process.
elements.sent-2411-022: We mitigate the potential for errors in LLMs by generating multiple solutions and selecting the correct process, with diversity playing a key role.
elements.sent-2411-023: Borrowing an idea from ReST~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2406-03816 , our SRA-MCTS approach leverages the diversity inherent in Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)~\cite{DBLP:conf/cg/Coulom06 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: As in Figure~\ref{fig:mcts , each node at a given layer represents a step in the solution process, our approach emphasizes the process more than CoT.}
elements.sent-2411-025: For a branch, if a child node is deemed incorrect, its selection probability is significantly reduced.
elements.sent-2411-026: We not only adjust the temperature coefficient for generation but also allow the model to see the previous response to generate the next one, avoiding repetitive answers.
elements.sent-2411-027: Additionally, the reward score evaluated by the large model is used to compute the UCB1 formula~\cite{DBLP:journals/ml/AuerCF02 , guiding the selection of the best response while maintaining the potential for exploration of other branches.}
elements.sent-2411-028: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:intro , using SRA-MCTS-generated data for fine-tuning results in a model that outperforms both the official version and the CoT fine-tuned model, achieving the best overall performance.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{mcts.png} \caption{The diagram illustrates our data generation process compared to other methods. In contrast to the popular Chain-of-Thought approach, our SRA-MCTS allows for more diverse reasoning. Additionally, through the Selection and Evaluation steps, we improve the quality of branch selection while reducing computational overhead.} \label{fig:mcts}
elements.sent-2411-030: We introduce SRA-MCTS, a plug-and-play data generation method for reasoning augmentation that is simple, effective, and enhances diversity while promoting autonomous thinking in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-031: We propose a pipeline for the code domain, where problems are processed by SRA-MCTS to generate diverse natural language plans, followed by the LLM converting these plans into code.
elements.sent-2411-032: The generated data is used for model fine-tuning, bringing the model's output closer to human-like thinking and enabling it to learn the reasoning process.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our method enables self-improvement in small models, for example, gemma2-2b, with experiments showing that the performance of self-improved small models surpasses that of small models trained with distilled data from a 70B model.
elements.sent-2411-034: Data distillation is an effective method for obtaining high-quality data, widely applied in the code generation field for question and answer distillation.
elements.sent-2411-035: For question distillation, CodeAlpaca~\cite{codealpaca uses self-instruct by modifying Standford Alpaca's prompts and seed tasks~\cite{alpaca , generating 20K instruction-following data points from GPT.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Evol-Instruct~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/LuoX0SGHT0LJ24 adds heuristic rules like reasoning difficulty using GPT to increase question's depth and diversity of CodeAlpaca.}
elements.sent-2411-037: OSS-Instruct~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2312-02120 leverages widely available open-source code snippets, designing prompts that enable GPT to extract corresponding questions from a few lines of code, further aligning the questions with real-world distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-038: CodeOcean~\cite{DBLP:conf/acl/YuZSHXZHY24 utilizes an LLM-based generator-discriminator approach, using GPT-3.5 to distill questions from open-source code snippets and GPT-4 to filter the outputs, yielding high-quality and diverse data.}
elements.sent-2411-039: From the perspective of answers, Phi-1~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2306-11644 distills large amounts of textbook content from GPT-3.5 to enhance the problem-solving process, achieving strong performance with smaller models.}
elements.sent-2411-040: DolphCoder~\cite{DBLP:conf/acl/Wang0DWZDXWZC24 generates step-by-step natural language CoT answers through multiple rounds of GPT generation, simplifying the learning process for large models.}
elements.sent-2411-041: OpenAI o1~\cite{openai_learning_to_reason uses CoT to simulate deep human-like thinking when solving problems.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Previous methods typically generate responses either as direct answers or through Chain-of-Thought (CoT).
elements.sent-2411-043: Direct answers have proven ineffective for complex problems~\cite{DBLP:conf/nips/Wei0SBIXCLZ22 , while CoT suffers from irreversible errors in intermediate steps.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Although step-by-step execution of CoT and adjusting temperature can alleviate some errors~\cite{DBLP:conf/acl/Wang0DWZDXWZC24 , its efficiency still lags behind the diversity offered by a tree structure.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The CoT-SC~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/0002WSLCNCZ23 method generates multiple answers and selects the most frequent one to mitigate errors, but the lack of diversity results in repetitive outputs, weakening error mitigation.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Our SRA-MCTS approach not only generates step-by-step reasoning using a tree structure but also enables error correction and optimal solution selection through MCTS's branch selection mechanism, effectively addressing the issue of insufficient diversity in answers.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.96\textwidth]{pipeline.png} \caption{The diagram shows the overall process of our method, with data generation on the left and training on the right. SRA-MCTS guides the LLM to generate a plan without code, which is then followed by the LLM generating the corresponding code. The Question, Plan, and Code are organized to create the training data for model fine-tuning. For simplicity, in SRA-MCTS only the steps where the LLM plays a key role are shown; other steps are explained later in the text.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-047: We propose a pipeline that leverages the model itself to enhance training data and improve performance, consisting of three stages: SRA-MCTS for plan generation, plan-to-code transformation, and training.
elements.sent-2411-048: By providing natural language descriptions for code, we enhance the model's performance in code generation, as shown in the Figure~\ref{fig:pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-049: For a set of problems, we first use the LLM with SRA-MCTS to generate natural language, step-by-step solutions for each problem (excluding code, except for necessary formulas).
elements.sent-2411-050: The LLM then generates specific code based on the problem and the generated solutions.
elements.sent-2411-051: Finally, we combine the problem, the natural language solution, and the code to create a fine-tuning dataset for training the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-052: We propose a Self-guided MCTS-based data generation method for Reasoning Augmentation, called SRA-MCTS.
elements.sent-2411-053: It consists of four phases: Selection, Expansion, Evaluation \& Reflection, and Backpropagation.
elements.sent-2411-054: These phases are repeated multiple times, with each repetition representing one iteration that generates a specific reasoning step.
elements.sent-2411-055: The LLM plays a key role in the middle two phases, while the first and last phases are guided by formulas or rules.
elements.sent-2411-056: By introducing sufficient diversity through a tree structure, LLM is able to fully stimulate its reasoning capabilities using the MCTS method.
elements.sent-2411-057: First, we need to select a leaf node from the tree structure as the context for the LLM’s reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-058: Starting from the root node, we recursively select the node with the highest UCB1~\cite{DBLP:journals/ml/AuerCF02 value at each branch (randomly if values are tied) until we reach a leaf node.}
elements.sent-2411-059: It is important to note that, during the first iteration, the root node, which also serves as a leaf node containing the problem, is selected by default.
elements.sent-2411-060: The chosen node will be used in the next step for the LLM’s plan generation.
elements.sent-2411-061: The selection criterion is based on the traditional UCB1 formula. \begin{equation}UCB1_i = r_i + c \cdot \sqrt{\frac {2 \cdot \log{N}}{n_i}} \label{Eq:selection}\end{equation} where $r_i$ is the reward score of the current plan at this node (generated by the LLM during evaluation), $c$ is the exploration constant, set to 0.5, $n_i$ is the number of visits to this node, $N$ is the number of visits to its parent node.
elements.sent-2411-062: We recursively select the node with the highest UCB1 value (randomly in case of ties) until reaching a leaf node.
elements.sent-2411-063: The selected node will then be used for the next step of plan generation by the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-064: In the previous phase, the selected leaf node $N_d$ will be expanded by the LLM to generate a new node representing a new plan.
elements.sent-2411-065: The new node’s state is formed by concatenating the $N_d$ ’s State and Action: \begin{equation}S_{d+1}^i = concatenate(S_d, A_d) \label{Eq:concatenate}\end{equation} where $d$ is the depth of the node in the current tree, $i$ represents the $i$-th branch.
elements.sent-2411-066: This concatenated state $S_{d+1}$ becomes the context for the LLM’s reasoning, guiding the model by showing the previous generation and adjusting the temperature parameter to control the model's diversity in its generation.
elements.sent-2411-067: The process is described by the following formula:\begin{equation}A_{d+1}^i = \begin{cases} \begin{aligned} LLM(S_{d+1}^i; R), \text{i = 1} LLM(S_{d+1}^i + A_{d+1}^{1} + ... + A_{d+1}^{i-1}; R), \text{i > 1} \end{aligned} \end{cases} \label{Eq:expansion}\end{equation} In this step, $A_{d+1 ^{i-1}$ refers to the output generated by the LLM in the previous branch, serving as a part of prompt to guide the model to generate non-redundant ideas.}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}A_{d+1}^i =   LLM(S_{d+1}^i; R), i = 1 LLM(S_{d+1}^i + A_{d+1}^{1} + ... + A_{d+1}^{i-1}; R), i > 1   \label{Eq:expansion}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-068: To manage computational costs, we set the number of expanded branches to 3.
elements.sent-2411-069: To avoid duplicate outputs, we perform a redundancy check.
elements.sent-2411-070: If any output is found to be repetitive, the generation process will be restarted.
elements.sent-2411-071: At this stage, code generation is not involved—only the natural language reasoning process is generated.
elements.sent-2411-072: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:Evaluation , We use the LLM to assign reward scores to all newly generated nodes from several perspectives: single-step correctness, overall solution coherence, solution completeness, and overall correctness.}
elements.sent-2411-073: If erroneous steps occur in the intermediate stages, the node will receive a lower reward score, reducing the likelihood of that branch being selected.
elements.sent-2411-074: This helps mitigate the loss of robustness commonly seen in chain-based reasoning due to mistakes in intermediate steps.
elements.sent-2411-075: The reward scoring is as follows: \begin{equation}r_{d+1} = LLM(S_{d+1} + A_{d+1}) \label{Eq:reward}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-076: During the Expansion phase, although the LLM generates new nodes, directly using them as input for the next step often results in unstable performance.
elements.sent-2411-077: To address this, we employ a reflection mechanism~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2406-03816 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: The model first generates a brief reflection based on the previous context, suggesting the approach for the next step.
elements.sent-2411-079: The LLM then generates the specific content for the next step based on this guidance.
elements.sent-2411-080: The reflection can also be used to assess whether the generated step can solve the problem.
elements.sent-2411-081: If the previous steps are deemed sufficient to solve the problem, the model outputs an <end> tag to indicate that the data generation process has concluded.
elements.sent-2411-082: The reflection value $R = LLM(S_{d+1 + A_{d+1 )$ is determined as follows: \begin{equation} R = \begin{cases} <end>, \text{problem solved} [1em] \text{short thought for next step}, \text{problem unsolved} \end{cases} \label{Eq:reflection}\end{equation} .}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}R =  <end>, problem solved [1em] short thought for next step, problem unsolved  \label{Eq:reflection}\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{evaluation.png} \caption{Evaluation: Each newly generated $Step_{n+1}$ from the expansion phase is combined with the problem and previous steps to form a $Plan$ (potentially incomplete) and then evaluated to generate a reward score.} \label{fig:Evaluation}
elements.sent-2411-083: Backpropagation recursively transmits reward increments from the leaf node to its parent nodes, allowing for more accurate selection in the next iteration.
elements.sent-2411-084: Early in the process, a plan may not receive a high score, but after a few iterations, it may become more reliable and thus should have an increased probability of being selected.
elements.sent-2411-085: This is achieved by propagating the increments from child nodes upward.
elements.sent-2411-086: The formula is as follows: \begin{subequations \renewcommand{\theequation {\theparentequation.\alph{equation \begin{equation}0.9{$ r_{increment} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{branch} Visits_{children} \ \cdot \ r_{children}}{\sum_{i=1}^{branch} Visits_{children}} $} \label{Eq:backpropogation-a} [1em] 0.9{$ r_{parent} = \alpha \cdot r_{parent} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot r_{increment} $} \label{Eq:backpropogation-b}\end{equation} \end{subequations where Visits represents the number of times a node is visited, used to balance the contribution of frequently visited nodes to the increment.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}0.9{$ r_{increment} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{branch} Visits_{children} \ \cdot \ r_{children}}{\sum_{i=1}^{branch} Visits_{children}} $} \label{Eq:backpropogation-a} [1em] 0.9{$ r_{parent} = \alpha \cdot r_{parent} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot r_{increment} $} \label{Eq:backpropogation-b}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-088: Repeat the four phases above until the <end> tag is output, or the iteration limit (either iteration count or time) is reached.
elements.sent-2411-089: Through SRA-MCTS, we obtain a batch of natural language plans.
elements.sent-2411-090: Now, we have the LLM act as a function implementer to strictly convert these plans into code, i.e., $Code = LLM(Problem + Plan)$.
elements.sent-2411-091: Below is the prompt for implementing the conversion: \begin{tcolorbox [colback=blue!5!white, colframe=blue!75!black, title=Prompt] You will play the role of a code implementer, writing a complete code based on the given problem and the step-by-step analysis of the problem.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Your code must strictly follow the analysis steps provided and should not include your own opinions.. \end{tcolorbox.}
elements.sent-2411-093: We have collected the Question, Plan, and Code triples, with the Question as input and the latter two as output, forming the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-094: Since our method emphasizes self-driven generation, unless stated otherwise, the datasets used for model training are self-generated.
elements.sent-2411-095: For training, we use LLaMA-Factory~\cite{zheng2024llamafactory for LoRA~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/HuSWALWWC22 fine-tuning.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: We select gemma-2-2b-it~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2408-00118 , Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2407-21783 , and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct~\cite{qwen2.5 as our baseline.}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: The selection of these models is based on their strong instruction-following capabilities and their extensive pre-training on large-scale downstream code data.
elements.sent-2411-098: This makes them well-suited for leveraging reasoning-augmented approaches to activate autonomous problem-solving abilities in programming tasks.
elements.sent-2411-099: The challenge in collecting suitable problems lies in ensuring that the problems are not overly simple, but rather have enough depth to allow for thorough analysis and exploration.
elements.sent-2411-100: Therefore, we select the LeetCode dataset~\cite{greengerong_leetcode , focusing on medium and hard problems.}
elements.sent-2411-101: We retain only the problem descriptions, discarding the solutions.
elements.sent-2411-102: To prevent overlap between the training and evaluation datasets, we perform decontamination on the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-103: This involved conducting a 10-gram-level duplication check for each problem, with a threshold set at 0.5.
elements.sent-2411-104: If the duplication rate exceeded this threshold, the corresponding problem was removed from the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-105: After decontamination, we obtain approximately 2,000 data samples.
elements.sent-2411-106: To evaluate the effectiveness of our approach, we use commonly adopted benchmarks in the code generation field: Human-Eval~\cite{chen2021codex and MBPP~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2108-07732 .}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Additionally, for a more accurate assessment, we include Human-Eval+, which contains 80 times the test cases of the original Human-Eval, and MBPP+~\cite{DBLP:conf/nips/LiuXW023 , which includes 35 times the test cases of the original MBPP.}
elements.sent-2411-108: The Human-Eval benchmarks involve code completion tasks, where the model is provided with the function name and comments and is tasked with completing the entire function.
elements.sent-2411-109: In contrast, MBPP benchmarks provide a brief functional requirement described in natural language, and the model must generate the function from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-110: In code generation, Pass@k is a common metric that measures the probability of generating a correct solution in at least one of $k$ attempts.
elements.sent-2411-111: It reflects the model's success rate across multiple attempts, which is especially useful given the uncertainty in the generation process.
elements.sent-2411-112: The Pass@k metric is defined as: \begin{equation}\text{pass@k} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{k} (1 - p_{i,j}) \right]\end{equation} where $N$ is the total number of test cases, $p_{i,j}$ represents the probability of success for the $j$-th generation in the $i$-th test case, $\prod_{j=1 ^{k (1 - p_{i,j )$ represents the probability that all $k$ generations for the $i$-th test case fail.}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Our experiments operate on the premise that training data is self-generated by the model.
elements.sent-2411-114: To test the validity of this approach, we use Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2407-21783 as an external model to generate data, which is then used to train smaller models.}
elements.sent-2411-115: We compare the performance of self-generated data with that of data distilled from a larger model to determine if the self-generation approach for small models can approximate the effectiveness of large-model distillation.
elements.sent-2411-116: To compare the performance of our SRA-MCTS method with CoT, a popular approach for reasoning enhancement, we evaluate it on the benchmarks mentioned earlier.
elements.sent-2411-117: We compare the results with original models as well as those trained explicitly using CoT.
elements.sent-2411-118: Except for the original model, the training data is self-generated.
elements.sent-2411-119: In the CoT group, the data is generated by prompting the model to create CoT-formatted data in a single generation.
elements.sent-2411-120: In contrast, our method directly uses SRA-MCTS to generate the data.

elements.paragraph-2411-003: SRA-MCTS enhances small models' reasoning, allowing self-generated data to outperform 70B-distilled data in improving performance.
elements.sent-2411-121: The results in Figure~\ref{fig:ablation indicate that self-synthesized data outperformed data synthesized by Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct across all model sizes and nearly all benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-122: SRA-MCTS demonstrates significant improvements on the Human-Eval and Human-Eval+ benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-123: In the 2B category, the SRA-MCTS method achieves an average increase of 2 points compared to data synthesized by the 70B model.
elements.sent-2411-124: The 8B category shows similar gains of over 2 points.
elements.sent-2411-125: Only at the 14B level does the data generated by the 70B model begin to show slight advantages on about half of the benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-126: Researcher's exploration of small model potential is still limited.
elements.sent-2411-127: Although 70B models are near the data synthesis upper limit for most researchers, experiments show that self-synthesized data from small models not only costs less but also rivals or surpasses that of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-128: This suggests that as model and data scale reach their limits, researchers should focus on unlocking small model potential and improving data quality.
elements.paragraph-2411-004: SRA-MCTS surpasses CoT in average performance and excels in pass@10, highlighting diversity.

elements.sent-2411-152: Our goal is to enable models to generate correct plans and autonomously guide their reasoning paths to solve problems effectively through data generation and training.

elements.paragraph-2411-007: Limited Self-Evaluation Capabilities of Small Models
elements.sent-2411-162: During the data generation process, the model is required to evaluate the generated content.
elements.sent-2411-163: However, small models often struggle with this task, failing to follow instructions and generating responses to the posed questions instead of evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-164: Even when scores are generated, they may be interspersed with irrelevant content, necessitating the use of manually crafted rules for extraction.
elements.sent-2411-165: To address the evaluation challenges posed by small models, approaches such as the popular LLM-as-a-judge framework or the Process Reward Model (PRM) can be employed.
elements.sent-2411-166: These methods involve fine-tuning the evaluation model to standardize its output direction and format.
elements.paragraph-2411-008: Dependence on Manual Hyperparameter Tuning in MCTS
elements.sent-2411-167: In the backpropagation of reward scores, the updates are propagated step by step to parent nodes based on manually defined proportions.
elements.sent-2411-168: During branch selection, manually set constants influence the exploration of lower-scoring branches.
elements.sent-2411-169: These constants need to be manually controlled within a reasonable range; significant deviations from this range may result in performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-170: MCTS has been proposed for many years, and numerous improvements to its algorithmic structure have been made during this time.
elements.sent-2411-171: Addressing the dependency on hyperparameters is undoubtedly a critical aspect of these advancements.
elements.sent-2411-172: In the future, we aim to modify these formulas to reduce reliance on manually defined parameters.
elements.sent-2411-173: In the data generation process, large models require multiple inferences for both generation and evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-174: To ensure non-redundant outputs, the model must reference previously generated content, making the entire process sequential and further impacting time efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-175: Inference acceleration frameworks, such as VLLM~\cite{kwon2023efficient , can be employed to speed up the inference process.}
elements.sent-2411-176: To address the issue of sequential processing, model diversity can be enhanced to avoid generating duplicate content, allowing the workflow to revert to a parallel process.
elements.sent-2411-177: This approach boosts performance across model scales, surpassing the official Instruct version and the traditional CoT method at the 8B and 14B levels.
elements.sent-2411-178: Our method enables self-improvement in small models, with experiments showing that the performance of self-improved small models surpasses that of small models trained with distilled data from a 70B model.
elements.sent-2411-179: Large language Models (LLMs) perform well on simple and medium-level problems in the field of code generation, but the performance on more complex problems is not satisfactory~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/LuoX0SGHT0LJ24,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2305-10679 .}
elements.sent-2411-180: The previous work aims to guide the LLMs’ reasoning process toward generating correct results through natural language as an intermediary.
elements.sent-2411-181: ScaleAI~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2409-03733 provides concrete experimental validation for previous work on answers that providing LLMs with correct solutions in natural language as a part of the answer, even if incomplete (just 10-20 tokens), can substantially boost the performance on benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-182: This demonstrates that providing solutions to LLMs can guide and inspire their reasoning process, and the correctness of the solution directly impacts the accuracy of the final result.
elements.sent-2411-183: Our code and data are public at \href{https://github.com/DIRECT-BIT/SRA-MCTS {https://github.com/DIRECT-BIT/SRA-MCTS.}}
elements.sent-2411-184: Large language models exhibit remarkable performance in simple code generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-185: However, they encounter significant challenges when addressing complex problems that require reasoning and question decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-186: To tackle this, we propose a self-driven reasoning augmentation process, SRA-MCTS, which incorporates Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for reasoning data generation.
elements.sent-2411-187: SRA-MCTS enables LLMs to self-generate intermediate reasoning steps and perform iterative self-evaluation, facilitating self-improvement.
elements.sent-2411-188: Specifically, it utilizes MCTS to produce diverse intermediate reasoning steps.
elements.sent-2411-189: During each iteration, MCTS generates a step and employs self-evaluation to guide the selection of subsequent branches, ultimately forming a sufficiently diverse reasoning path referred to as “thinking”.
elements.sent-2411-190: This thinking guides the model in generating corresponding code, and both are combined as training data for supervised fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-191: Experimental results demonstrate that SRA-MCTS achieves consistent performance improvements across three model scales without additional supervisory assistance.
elements.sent-2411-192: Applied to the Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model, it delivers an 11-point improvement on the MBPP-Complex dataset, underscoring the significant potential for model self-improvement.

elements.sent-2411-193: Large language models (LLMs) excel at generating code for simple questions, but their performance on more complex tasks remains unsatisfactory.~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/LuoX0SGHT0LJ24,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2305-10679 .}
elements.sent-2411-194: Recent studies have predominantly concentrated on enhancing the quality of training datasets to improve model performance.
elements.sent-2411-195: These improvements typically address two fundamental aspects of the training data.
elements.sent-2411-196: On one hand, improving the quality of the questions enhances the model's understanding of the input.
elements.sent-2411-197: Some methods optimize the question portion of the dataset by leveraging more powerful models or summarizing existing open-source code, thereby enhancing both the depth and breadth of the questions~\cite{DBLP:conf/nips/Ouyang0JAWMZASR22,DBLP:conf/iclr/LuoX0SGHT0LJ24,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2312-02120 .}
elements.sent-2411-198: On the other hand, increasing the complexity of the answers helps guide the model to generate higher-quality codes.
elements.sent-2411-199: BRAINSTORM~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2305-10679 introduces a brainstorming approach to generate and select diverse ideas related to a given question, thereby enhancing the model's reasoning capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-200: DolphCoder~\cite{DBLP:conf/acl/Wang0DWZDXWZC24 utilizes GPT-3.5 with multiple prompt templates to obtain diverse outputs for multi-objective instruction fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-201: These methods all aim to leverage additional natural language text to guide LLMs in generating high-quality results.
elements.sent-2411-202: Furthermore, ScaleAI~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2409-03733 has demonstrated that providing LLMs with correct solutions in natural language form can significantly enhance model performance, even when these solutions consist of incomplete plans with only a few dozen tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-203: This highlights the potential of natural language solutions to guide and inspire LLMs to think in the right direction.
elements.sent-2411-204: Similar to ScaleAI, there have been some works exploring how to improve the intermediate reasoning steps to improve model performance.~\cite{DBLP:conf/cvpr/0003XKISGX24,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2409-09584,DBLP:conf/acl/LongWXZDCW24 .}
elements.sent-2411-205: However, these studies lack explanations for the natural language solutions and lack an inherent mechanism for error correction, limiting the model's ability to effectively utilize natural language reasoning steps.
elements.sent-2411-206: Therefore, we focus on the intermediate natural language reasoning steps generated by the model, providing it with a reliable and diverse reasoning direction.
elements.sent-2411-207: In this paper, we refer to the intermediate natural language reasoning steps generated by the model for code generation as ``thinking''.
elements.sent-2411-208: We propose a Self-driven Reasoning Augmentation method, leveraging Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)~\cite{DBLP:conf/cg/Coulom06 to generate high-quality thinking, which we call SRA-MCTS as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:pipeline .}}
elements.sent-2411-209: Different from ReST-MCTS*~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2406-03816 , which uses MCTS to generate complete answers, we apply MCTS to the more critical thinking portion.}
elements.sent-2411-210: This provides the model with reliable reasoning, enhancing its ability to generate high-quality code.
elements.sent-2411-211: SRA-MCTS iteratively constructs the final reasoning path for a given question.
elements.sent-2411-212: Specifically, each iteration generates multiple potential next steps based on the current reasoning path.
elements.sent-2411-213: During each step selection, the model reflects on and scores the new generated step, updating the scores of previously generated steps.
elements.sent-2411-214: This process allows the model to re-evaluate and reconsider unselected steps in subsequent iterations.
elements.sent-2411-215: This iterative process enables the model to engage in self-reflection, correcting potentially flawed reasoning steps.
elements.sent-2411-216: Finally, the sequence of reasoning steps generated over multiple iterations is combined into comprehensive thinking that guides the model in generating the final code.
elements.sent-2411-217: The data generated through SRA-MCTS will be used to fine-tune the model, ultimately resulting in a higher-performing code generation model.
elements.sent-2411-218: We propose a plug-and-play reasoning augmentation data generation method, SRA-MCTS.
elements.sent-2411-219: This method is simple and effective, as it enables the LLM to self-generate and self-evaluate data during the process.
elements.sent-2411-220: Then the generated data is used for model training, fostering the model’s self-improvement.
elements.sent-2411-221: We propose a pipeline for the code generation domain, where input questions are processed by SRA-MCTS to generate diverse, high-quality thinking without additional supervision, thereby creating a positive feedback loop for continuous improvement.
elements.sent-2411-222: We perform a detailed analysis of the generated data, examining the impact of its quality on model performance.
elements.sent-2411-223: Additionally, by comparing the model’s performance on the original benchmark and the derived, more complex benchmarks, we validate the effectiveness of the self reasoning augmentation approach.

elements.sent-2411-225: OSS-Instruct~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2312-02120 leverages open-source code snippets to generate questions that better represent real-world distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-226: CodeOcean~\cite{DBLP:conf/acl/YuZSHXZHY24 utilizes GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 for question distillation and filtering, resulting in high-quality, diverse data.}
elements.sent-2411-227: DolphCoder~\cite{DBLP:conf/acl/Wang0DWZDXWZC24 improves LLM learning by generating step-by-step answers through multiple turns and pseudo code for problem-solving.}

elements.sent-2411-231: Similar to how humans think deeply before answering difficult questions, OpenAI o1~\cite{openai_learning_to_reason employs a series of thought processes to solve problems.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Through reinforcement learning, it refines its strategies, corrects errors, and streamlines complex steps.
elements.sent-2411-233: AFLOW~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2410-10762 employs MCTS to refine workflows through iterative code modifications and feedback.}

elements.sent-2411-235: Unlike previous works, our SRA-MCTS not only enhances the thinking process within the data, improving the model’s reasoning and thinking capabilities, but also establishes a fully autonomous pipeline that includes self-generation, self-evaluation, and ultimately self-improvement.
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{new_mcts.png} \caption{Self-driven reasoning augmentation process of SRA-MCTS. (a) Selection: A leaf node is selected to be expanded in the next phase. (b) Expansion: A single step is generated and assigned to the node. (c) Evaluation \& Reflection: The step in the node is scored and an insight is generated as reflection. (d) Backpropagation: Reward scores are propagated back. In the notation ``1-1'' within a node, the first ``1'' indicates that it is the 1-st step in the thinking, and the second ``1'' denotes the 1-st variant for this step. The same logic applies to other nodes. \textcolor{blue}{Blue} nodes represent the selected nodes, and \textcolor{red}{red} nodes represent newly expanded nodes.} \label{fig:mcts}
elements.sent-2411-236: We propose a pipeline that leverages the model itself to enhance training data and augment reasoning ability, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:pipeline , consisting of three stages: SRA-MCTS, Thinking to code, and Training.}
elements.sent-2411-237: First, we use the LLM with SRA-MCTS to generate step-by-step natural language thinking for each question.
elements.sent-2411-238: Then, the LLM generates specific codes based on the question and the generated thinking.
elements.sent-2411-239: Finally, we combine the question, the thinking, and the code to create a fine-tuning dataset for training the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-240: We develop a self-driven reasoning augmentation method with MCTS, called SRA-MCTS, to generate natural language thinking for questions.
elements.sent-2411-241: It consists of four phases: Selection, Expansion, Evaluation \& Reflection, and Backpropagation, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:mcts .}
elements.sent-2411-242: These phases are carried out on a search tree composed of tree nodes and will be repeated multiple times, with each iteration generating a specific reasoning step.
elements.sent-2411-243: A tree node has four attributes: 1) State represents the current state, including the question and the steps generated so far; 2) Action represents the next step generated based on the current state; 3) Reward represents the score generated by LLM based on state and action; 4) Reflection guides the generation of the next iteration.
elements.sent-2411-244: The initial search tree consists of only a root node, with its state attribute set to the input question, and the other three attributes are empty.
elements.sent-2411-245: The purpose of the selection is to select one step from the model's currently generated steps for the next step generation.
elements.sent-2411-246: The selection phase involves selecting a leaf node $N_d$ from the search tree, where $d$ represents the depth of the leaf node.
elements.sent-2411-247: This node will be expanded in the expansion phase.
elements.sent-2411-248: We use UCB1~\cite{DBLP:journals/ml/AuerCF02 , which is calculated based on the node's reward value, to measure the value of each node being selected.}
elements.sent-2411-249: The formula is as follows: \begin{equation}UCB1 = r + c \cdot \sqrt{\frac {2 \cdot \ln{N}}{n}} \label{Eq:selection}\end{equation} where $r$ is the reward value of the current node that is generated by the Evaluation \& Reflection phase in the last iteration, $c$ is the exploration constant set to 0.5, $N$ denotes the number of times the parent node has been selected, and $n$ represents the number of times the current node has been selected.
elements.sent-2411-250: UCB1 is designed to balance exploitation and exploration, represented by the terms on the left and right of the formula respectively.
elements.sent-2411-251: If the exploration term is removed, it degenerates into a greedy selection, which harms diversity.
elements.sent-2411-252: With the selection phase starting from the root node, the node with the highest UCB1 value at the current depth is selected.
elements.sent-2411-253: Then, the process continues by choosing the child node with the highest UCB1 value among all the child nodes of the selected node.
elements.sent-2411-254: This process continues until a leaf node of the search tree is selected.
elements.sent-2411-255: Note that during the first iteration, only the root node which is also a leaf node exists, so the root node's UCB1 value does not need to be calculated.
elements.sent-2411-256: The purpose of the expansion is to generate the next step based on the existing steps.
elements.sent-2411-257: The expansion phase involves generating several new child nodes based on the selected node $N_d$, and assigning values to the state and action attributes of the new nodes.
elements.sent-2411-258: The state attribute is designed to store the input question and steps that the LLM has generated up to the current node.
elements.sent-2411-259: Therefore, for child nodes that originate from the same parent node, their state values are identical and referred to $S_{d+1}$, as they share the input question and the same history of step.
elements.sent-2411-260: Specifically, $S_{d+1}$ is formed by concatenating the state and action attributes of the node $N_d$: \begin{equation}S_{d+1} = concatenate(S_d, A_d) \label{Eq:concatenate}\end{equation} where $S_d$ and $A_d$ represent the state and action of the selected node $N_d$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-261: The action attribute is designed to store the next step generated based on the current state and the reflection of the parent node.
elements.sent-2411-262: Since different methods can be used to generate different next steps, the action attribute can have multiple distinct values, leading to the creation of different child nodes.
elements.sent-2411-263: Each node represents a possible path that can be made from the current state, allowing for the exploration of various possibilities in the search tree.
elements.sent-2411-264: To generate diversity in the steps, the expansion phase adopts the sample decoding strategy, where each next step is generated one by one.
elements.sent-2411-265: Each time a new step is generated, it represents a new node created from the selected node $N_d$.
elements.sent-2411-266: To prevent the LLM from generating duplicate next steps, we add the next steps that have already been generated to the input.
elements.sent-2411-267: This process can be represented by the following formula: \begin{equation}A_{d+1}^i = \begin{cases} \begin{aligned} LLM(S_{d+1}; R_{d}), \text{$i = 1$} LLM(S_{d+1}; A_{d+1}^{1} , ..., A_{d+1}^{i-1}; R_{d}), \text{$i > 1$} \end{aligned} \end{cases} \label{Eq:expansion}\end{equation} where $A_{d+1 ^{i}$ represents the action value of the $i$-th child node at depth $d$+1, $R_d$ represents the reflection attribute value of the selected node $N_d$, which is generated in the last iteration.}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}A_{d+1}^i =   LLM(S_{d+1}; R_{d}), $i = 1$ LLM(S_{d+1}; A_{d+1}^{1} , ..., A_{d+1}^{i-1}; R_{d}), $i > 1$   \label{Eq:expansion}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-268: Adding the already generated steps to the input helps the LLM explore different paths in the search space.
elements.sent-2411-269: To manage computational costs, we set the number of expanded nodes to 3.
elements.sent-2411-270: If a duplicate step is detected, the generation process is repeated, with a maximum of 5 retries At this stage, code generation is not involved, only the natural language reasoning process is generated.
elements.sent-2411-271: The third phase consists of two parts: Evaluation and Reflection, which support the selection and expansion phases, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-272: The evaluation part assigns the reward attribute values to the new nodes generated in the expansion phase.
elements.sent-2411-273: These reward values will be then used in the selection phase to calculate the UCB1 values.
elements.sent-2411-274: We use the LLM itself, which generates the steps during the expansion phase, as the evaluator to score the new nodes and explore its potential for self-improvement through self-evaluation.~\footnote{Note: The use of the model itself here is aimed at exploring the potential for end-to-end self-improvement.}
elements.sent-2411-275: Larger open-source models and closed-source model APIs, which may have more accurate evaluation capabilities, can easily replace this component.
elements.sent-2411-276: The reward value of the $i$-th new node $N_{d+1 ^{i}$ is as follows: \begin{equation}r_{d+1}^i = LLM(S_{d+1}^i, A_{d+1}^i) \label{Eq:reward}\end{equation} We use a progressive scoring method, making judgments sequentially from four aspects, the process is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:Evaluation .}}
elements.sent-2411-277: The order of judgment is as follows: Single-step correctness, Solution coherence, Solution completeness, and Solution correctness.
elements.sent-2411-278: If the criteria for the current aspect are met, the corresponding score is given directly, and the other aspects are ignored.
elements.sent-2411-279: The reflection part aims to guide the next generation direction for the current node, and assigns the reflection attribute values to the new nodes generated in the expansion phase.
elements.sent-2411-280: The reflection attribute is a brief thought generated by the LLM and will be used in the next iteration's expansion phase.
elements.sent-2411-281: We use the reflection~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2406-03816 mechanism to allow the model to think before generating the next action, thus preventing performance degradation.}
elements.sent-2411-282: The $reflection$ value of the $i$-th new node $N_{d+1 ^i$ is as follows: \begin{equation}R_{d+1}^i = LLM(S_{d+1}^i, A_{d+1}^i) \label{Eq:reflection-1}\end{equation} Additionally, the reflection value is used to assess whether all the generated steps have solved the question.}
elements.sent-2411-283: If the generated steps from state and action at this node are deemed sufficient to solve the question, the reflection value will be an $<end>$ tag to indicate that the SRA-MCTS process has concluded.
elements.sent-2411-284: Otherwise, the LLM will generate a reflection for the next iteration's reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-285: The reflection value is shown as follows: \begin{equation} R = \begin{cases} <end>, \text{question solved} [1em] \text{short thought for next step}, \text{question unsolved} \end{cases} \label{Eq:reflection-2}\end{equation} .
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}R =  <end>, question solved [1em] short thought for next step, question unsolved  \label{Eq:reflection-2}\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-010: [] \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{evaluation.png} \caption{The progressive scoring method. The state and action of the node are used as inputs, and the judgment is made sequentially from left to right based on the four principles. If the current principle is satisfied, an integer score in the corresponding interval is output; otherwise, the next principle is evaluated. If all the principles are not met, the model will give the current input a full score of 10.} \label{fig:Evaluation}
elements.sent-2411-286: Backpropagation aims to update the reward values of all parent nodes of the current node, making the reward values of the parent nodes more accurate, thereby affecting the generation of the thinking.
elements.sent-2411-287: For the new nodes generated during the expansion phase \{${N_{d+1 ^{1 , N_{d+1 ^{2 , ..., N_{d+1 ^I}$\}, they collectively update their parent node, which is the node selected in the selection phase $N_d$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-288: Afterward, the parent node $N_d$ continues to update its parent node $N_{d-1}$, and this process is repeated until the root node is updated.
elements.sent-2411-289: The update process of the parent node's reward value $r_p$ is as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} r_{\Delta} = \sum_{i=1}^{I} (v_{c}^i \ \cdot \ r_{c}^i) / \sum_{i=1}^{I} v_{c}^i r_{p} = \alpha \cdot r_{p} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot r_{\Delta} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $v_{c ^i$ represents the number of times the $i$-th child node is selected, $r_{c ^i$ represents the reward score of the $i$-th child node, $r_{\Delta}$ represents the weighted increment of the parent node's reward value, and $\alpha$ is a hyperparameter to balance the original reward of the parent node and the increment from the child nodes.}}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation} r_{\Delta} = \sum_{i=1}^{I} (v_{c}^i \ \cdot \ r_{c}^i) / \sum_{i=1}^{I} v_{c}^i r_{p} = \alpha \cdot r_{p} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot r_{\Delta} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-290: The updated reward value of the parent node will influence the selection of child nodes in subsequent iterations.
elements.sent-2411-291: Specifically, in the next selection phase, SRA-MCTS will use the updated reward values to evaluate the relative merits of different nodes, thereby deciding which branch to explore.
elements.sent-2411-292: This dynamic reward adjustment helps gradually refine the strategy across the entire tree, making the search more efficient and progressively converging toward the optimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-293: SRA-MCTS repeats the four phases above until the $<end>$ tag is output, or the iteration limit is reached.
elements.sent-2411-294: SRA-MCTS generates multiple steps based on the question, and these steps together form a thinking.
elements.sent-2411-295: In this stage, we have the LLM act as a function implementer, strictly converting the thinking into code.
elements.sent-2411-296: After the first two stages, we collect the question, thinking, and code triples, with the question as the input and the latter two as the output, forming the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-297: This dataset is then used for supervised fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-298: To validate whether SRA-MCTS can indeed generate higher-quality thinking, we need to collect questions with a certain degree of difficulty.
elements.sent-2411-299: Therefore, we select medium and hard-level questions from the LeetCode dataset~\cite{greengerong_leetcode , excluding easy-level questions.}
elements.sent-2411-300: We retain only the question descriptions and discard the answers, allowing different methods to generate both thinking and code.
elements.sent-2411-301: To prevent any overlap between the training and test sets, we perform decontamination on the training data.
elements.sent-2411-302: Specifically, we conduct 10-gram level duplicate detection on each question in the dataset, setting the threshold at 0.3.
elements.sent-2411-303: If the similarity between a training question and any test set question exceeds this threshold, we remove the corresponding training data.
elements.sent-2411-304: As a result, we obtain a final training set of 1,819 unique questions.
elements.sent-2411-305: For thinking and code generation, we use SRA-MCTS to generate the corresponding thinking for the questions in the training set.
elements.sent-2411-306: Each step in the generation process is performed in a zero-shot manner.
elements.sent-2411-307: We prompt the model to generate each step according to a specified format, ensuring that the steps can be easily extracted.
elements.sent-2411-308: If the thinking contains code, we use a regular expression to remove it, leaving only the natural language reasoning content.
elements.sent-2411-309: This ensures that the reasoning process is kept separate from the code, enabling a clearer focus on the thought process behind the code generation.
elements.sent-2411-310: After generating the final thinking, we concatenate the question with its corresponding thinking and prompt the model to generate the final code.
elements.sent-2411-311: We choose gemma-2-2b-it~\cite{team2024gemma , Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct~\cite{dubey2024llama , and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct~\cite{qwen2.5 as our backbone models.}}}
elements.sent-2411-312: The rationale behind this selection is their strong instruction-following capabilities and the fact that they have been pre-trained on extensive code data.
elements.sent-2411-313: These models demonstrate significant potential to activate proactive thinking for solving programming tasks through reasoning augmentation techniques.
elements.sent-2411-314: We directly use the backbone models to perform reasoning on the test set, which serves as the baseline performance score for comparison.
elements.sent-2411-315: We apply the Chain-of-Thought (CoT)~\cite{DBLP:conf/nips/Wei0SBIXCLZ22 and Tree-of-Thought (ToT)~\cite{DBLP:conf/nips/YaoYZS00N23 reasoning methods to each model as baseline methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-316: For CoT, we use the conventional prompt, ``Let's think step by step'', to generate the thinking.
elements.sent-2411-317: For ToT, we use depth-first search to generate the thinking, setting the maximum depth to 4 and the branching factor to 3, which aligns with the number of nodes expanded during the expansion phase of SRA-MCTS.
elements.sent-2411-318: Similar to SRA-MCTS, the thinking generated by these methods is processed using regular expressions to remove any code content.
elements.sent-2411-319: Finally, the generated thinking is concatenated with the question and fed into the model to produce the final code.
elements.sent-2411-320: We use commonly adopted benchmarks in the code generation field, including Human-Eval~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2107-03374 and MBPP~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2108-07732 .}}
elements.sent-2411-321: Additionally, we utilize Human-Eval+ and MBPP+ within the EvalPlus~\cite{DBLP:conf/nips/LiuXW023 framework, where the test cases are several times larger than the original versions.}
elements.sent-2411-322: As for the task type, Human-Eval involves completing a given function, while MBPP requires the model to generate the function from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-323: To evaluate the model's reasoning ability across different difficulty levels, we categorize the test set questions into different difficulty levels.
elements.sent-2411-324: Specifically, we use GPT-4o~\cite{hurst2024gpt to classify the questions into easy, medium, and hard categories.}
elements.sent-2411-325: All questions across the three difficulty levels form the Full split and the medium and hard questions are grouped into the Complex split.
elements.sent-2411-326: In code generation field, pass@k is a widely used metric that measures the probability of generating a correct solution in at least one of $k$ attempts~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2107-03374,DBLP:conf/nips/KulalPC0PAL19 .}
elements.sent-2411-327: It reflects the model's success rate across multiple attempts, which is particularly valuable given the inherent uncertainty in the generation process.
elements.sent-2411-328: In SRA-MCTS, the total iteration limit is set to 5, the generation temperature in the expansion phase is set to 0.9, with a top-p sampling value of 0.98, and the $\alpha$ value in the backpropagation phase is manually set to 0.5.
elements.sent-2411-329: We use the models mentioned in the baseline as the foundation for training, setting the learning rate to 1e-4, training for 2 epochs, with a warmup ratio of 0.05 and a batch size of 4.
elements.sent-2411-330: For each model scale, we train using three different datasets: 1) Self-generated CoT data; 2) Self-generated ToT data; 3) SRA-MCTS generated data.
elements.sent-2411-331: We use Llama-factory~\cite{zheng2024llamafactory for LoRA~\cite{DBLP:conf/iclr/HuSWALWWC22 fine-tuning, with the models trained on two RTX A6000 GPUs.}}
elements.paragraph-2411-018: SRA-MCTS demonstrates the best overall performance and excels in solving complex questions.
elements.sent-2411-332: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:main , our method achieves the highest average performance improvement across all models, with a significantly larger gain on the Complex split of most benchmarks compared to the Full split, particularly with an increase of approximately 2-15 points on MBPP and MBPP+.}
elements.sent-2411-333: However, for the Human-Eval-related benchmarks, all methods experience a decline in several metrics.
elements.sent-2411-334: While our method still shows improvement in some metrics, it is not obvious compared to the other baseline methods.
elements.sent-2411-335: We attribute this phenomenon to the differences between the task types in the training and test sets.
elements.sent-2411-336: As discussed in Section \ref{benchmark , tasks in the MBPP benchmark, like those in the training set, require the model to generate code from scratch based on a given question, whereas Human-Eval tasks ask the model to complete a function header provided in the prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-337: Due to the consistency in task style between MBPP and the training set, our method achieves more substantial improvements compared to other methods.
elements.sent-2411-338: This highlights the critical role that the style of training data plays in influencing downstream task performance.
elements.paragraph-2411-019: The larger the model, the more pronounced the improvement from our method.
elements.sent-2411-339: As we observe in Table~\ref{tab:main , the average performance improvement increases with the model size, and this trend is even more evident on the Complex split.}
elements.sent-2411-340: We attribute this to the fact that larger models possess better generation and evaluation capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-341: With stronger generation and evaluation capabilities enabled by larger model scales, these models can produce higher-quality data and more effectively identify errors and redundancies in reasoning steps.
elements.sent-2411-342: SRA-MCTS effectively leverages these enhanced capabilities, leading to significant performance gains.

elements.sent-2411-343: We additionally train a model on a dataset without thinking, containing only the question and code, and compare it with the dataset that includes the question, thinking, and code triples.
elements.sent-2411-344: For a fair comparison, the code in the dataset without thinking is directly extracted from the training set generated by SRA-MCTS.
elements.sent-2411-345: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:ablation , nearly all cases without thinking perform worse than those with thinking, with this gap being more obvious on the Complex split.}
elements.sent-2411-346: We believe this is due to simpler questions not requiring much reasoning from the model to provide the correct solution, allowing a code-only training set to solve these questions effectively.
elements.sent-2411-347: It is only when the question difficulty increases that the model benefits from higher-quality thinking to guide it toward generating the correct code.

elements.sent-2411-348: We extract the thinkings generated by CoT, ToT, and SRA-MCTS from their respective training sets and concatenate them with the question and ground truth code to create three new training sets, each differing only in the thinking content.
elements.sent-2411-349: We then retrain the backbone models on these new datasets, and the results are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:counterpart .}
elements.sent-2411-350: Our method generates the most stable thinking among the three approaches.
elements.sent-2411-351: This is demonstrated by the fact that even when the code is replaced, our method maintains strong performance, whereas the other methods experience noticeable performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-352: This indicates that the thinking generated by our method not only contributes to the final performance improvement but also provides robustness, leading to more stable model.

elements.sent-2411-353: We use GPT-4o to evaluate the quality of the thinking, categorizing each instance into low, medium, and high quality.
elements.sent-2411-354: The ratio of medium and high quality thinking in each training set are used to represent the quality score of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-355: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:scatter , there is generally a linear relationship between the quality of the thinking in the training set and the model's performance, meaning that higher-quality thinking leads to better final performance.}
elements.sent-2411-356: However, for our method, there are cases in the gemma and Qwen models where the thinking quality is relatively low, yet the performance is higher than expected.
elements.sent-2411-357: We hypothesize that, during the evaluation of thinking, the evaluator may overemphasize unnecessary overthinking steps, complicating the model's ability to generate code based on overly complex reasoning processes.
elements.sent-2411-358: This not only suggests that the evaluation of larger models may not always be reliable, but also highlights the superiority of our method.

elements.sent-2411-372: We propose a self-improvement pipeline where SRA-MCTS performs both self-generation and self-evaluation for given questions.
elements.sent-2411-373: The high-quality thinking text generated through this process serves to guide code generation, with both the thinking and the code subsequently used for fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-374: Experimental results demonstrate that SRA-MCTS outperforms both CoT and ToT on more complex tasks, achieving an 11-point improvement on the MBPP Complex split.. \bibliographystyle{named.}
